expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Normal to elevated; may show impaired fasting glucose (100–125 mg/dL) or diabetes (≥126 mg/dL) depending on obesity/insulin resistance severity
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Normal to elevated; may be in prediabetes range (5.7–6.4%) or diabetes range (≥6.5%)
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Often elevated (hyperinsulinemia) consistent with insulin resistance, especially with obesity
expected_lab_results.TSH: Normal or mildly elevated; central hypothyroidism possible (TSH may be normal/low-normal despite low FT4)
expected_lab_results.Free T4: Normal or low; low FT4 with inappropriately normal/low TSH suggests central hypothyroidism (a known PWS complication)
expected_lab_results.Total testosterone (morning): Low for age/sex in males (or low-normal) consistent with hypogonadism
expected_lab_results.LH: Low or inappropriately normal (hypogonadotropic pattern common in PWS)
expected_lab_results.FSH: Low or inappropriately normal (hypogonadotropic pattern common in PWS)

non_pharmacological: Structured hyperphagia/obesity management: strict environmental food security, calorie-controlled diet with caregiver involvement, regular scheduled meals, avoidance of unrestricted access to food

Screen and manage PWS-associated comorbidities: sleep-disordered breathing (sleep study if symptoms), scoliosis, behavioral/psychiatric issues, skin picking, and developmental supports

Thinks factor XIII is only needed for surgery or childbirth, so routine prophylaxis is unnecessary if she feels okay.
Assumes bruising or delayed bleeding after minor injuries is due to being "anemic" or "having thin blood" from diet, not the congenital condition.
Believes taking iron supplements or eating leafy greens can replace the need for factor treatment.
Thinks she can "build tolerance" by skipping doses and only treating when bleeding happens.
Fears that factor products are likely to transmit infections and that newer products are "experimental."
Believes hormonal contraception will automatically stop all bleeding risks and make factor treatment optional.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 27-year-old warehouse shift supervisor with a vague, evasive style. Start with: “I can’t get this bleeding to stop after a tooth pull.” Volunteer that it was okay at first, then restarted hours later; you bruise easily; nosebleeds can last long; today you feel tired/lightheaded. Minimize details unless directly asked; answer briefly and a bit guarded.

If asked about bleeding history, disclose: cuts re-bleed later; heavy periods with clots; newborn belly-button bleeding per parents; prior surgery bled more than expected and kept returning; miscarriages; “usual clotting tests were normal.”

Withhold unless specifically probed: deep expanding bruises/limb swelling after minor bumps, bad headaches after minor head hits, gum bleeding so you avoid brushing/flossing, avoiding sports/dentist, cousin with “weird bleeding.”

Resist/avoid discussing post-sex bleeding and rectal bleeding; admit only with gentle, specific questioning. Mention you overuse extra-strength pain relievers and worry it worsens bleeding.

Express misconceptions: herbal teas/supplements “thin my blood,” dentist “messed up,” maybe von Willebrand/scurvy.

If clinician shows empathy, soften slightly but stay worried about insurance, job, time off, and costs of home factor.

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (recombinant, catridecacog) 35 IU/kg IV every 28 days
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day
Tranexamic acid 1300 mg PO three times daily as needed for heavy menstrual bleeding (up to 5 days per cycle)
Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain
confounders: Normal PT/INR and aPTT despite significant bleeding tendency (can delay recognition of a coagulation disorder)
History of heavy menstrual bleeding can be misattributed to gynecologic causes (e.g., fibroids) rather than a rare bleeding disorder
Intermittent NSAID use for dysmenorrhea (increases bleeding risk and may be mistaken as primary cause)
Iron deficiency anemia with tachycardia can mimic other causes of fatigue/palpitations and complicate assessment of bleeding severity

correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital factor XIII deficiency
acceptable_alternatives: Congenital FXIII deficiency with bleeding diathesis
required_evidence: Bleeding history consistent with FXIII deficiency (e.g., delayed bleeding after trauma/surgery, umbilical stump bleeding in infancy, poor wound healing, recurrent soft-tissue bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage history/family history)
Normal platelet count and normal red cell indices unless iron deficiency from chronic blood loss
Routine coagulation tests often normal in FXIII deficiency (PT/aPTT typically normal; note: not provided in available labs)
Confirmation with FXIII activity assay and/or FXIII antigen testing and/or genetic testing (acknowledge as required confirmatory testing beyond provided labs)

expected_lab_results.CBC - hemoglobin: Often normal unless chronic bleeding; may be low if iron-deficiency anemia from recurrent bleeding
expected_lab_results.CBC - hematocrit: Often normal; may be low if anemia present
expected_lab_results.CBC - MCV: Normal; may be low (microcytosis) if iron deficiency develops
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Normal or mildly elevated if recent significant bleeding; typically not markedly elevated unless acute blood loss
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Normal; may be low if iron deficiency from chronic bleeding
expected_lab_results.Serum iron: Normal; may be low if iron deficiency from chronic bleeding

medications: Factor XIII concentrate (plasma-derived or recombinant) 35 IU/kg IV every 28 days for prophylaxis (adjust to maintain trough FXIII activity per hematology)
Factor XIII concentrate 35-50 IU/kg IV once for acute bleeding or peri-procedural coverage (dose/interval guided by FXIII activity and clinical severity)
Tranexamic acid 10-15 mg/kg PO/IV every 8 hours as adjunct for mucosal bleeding or dental procedures (if no contraindications)
Ferrous sulfate 325 mg PO every other day if iron deficiency confirmed (low ferritin/iron with compatible indices)
non_pharmacological: Urgent hematology consultation for confirmatory testing (FXIII activity/antigen) and individualized prophylaxis/target trough planning
Develop a written bleeding/emergency plan and provide medical alert identification indicating FXIII deficiency
Avoid medications that increase bleeding risk when possible (e.g., NSAIDs/aspirin) unless specifically indicated and risk-assessed
Peri-procedural planning: notify surgical/dental teams in advance; arrange FXIII replacement and monitoring
Family screening/genetic counseling as appropriate for inherited condition
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks to confirm diagnosis and establish prophylaxis; then every 3-6 months (or sooner after bleeding episodes/procedures) with trough FXIII activity monitoring per specialist

must_include: FXIII deficiency is a rare inherited bleeding disorder where clots form but are not stabilized, causing delayed/recurrent bleeding
Routine clotting tests can be normal; confirmatory FXIII activity testing is needed
Risk of serious bleeding (including intracranial hemorrhage) and importance of having an emergency plan
Mainstay is FXIII replacement (concentrate) for prevention and for treatment around bleeding/surgery
Prophylaxis schedule (typically every ~4 weeks) and need for hematology-directed dosing/monitoring
Adjunct options (e.g., tranexamic acid) for certain bleeding types and when to use them
When to seek urgent care (head injury, severe headache, neurologic symptoms, heavy bleeding, post-procedure bleeding)
Discuss infusion-related risks (hypersensitivity) and signs to report immediately
Discuss thrombosis risk is generally low but assess individual risk factors, especially if using antifibrinolytics
Clarify penicillin allergy relevance (not directly related to FXIII products) and ensure it is documented
Avoid high-risk activities for head trauma when possible; use protective gear
Avoid NSAIDs/aspirin unless clinician-approved; use acetaminophen for pain/fever when appropriate
Carry medical alert ID and keep a copy of the treatment/emergency plan; inform all clinicians/dentists

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit bleeding history (delayed bleeding, umbilical stump bleeding, mucosal bleeding), prior surgeries/dental work outcomes, family history/consanguinity, current meds (NSAIDs/anticoagulants), pregnancy status if relevant, and red flags (head trauma, severe headache, neuro symptoms).

Order confirmatory testing for suspected congenital FXIII deficiency (FXIII activity assay; consider antigen/inhibitor testing if low/atypical), plus baseline CBC/fibrinogen as supportive context; acknowledge PT/aPTT may be normal.
Review FXIII activity results to confirm diagnosis and assess severity; ensure no alternative explanation for bleeding pattern.
Make/confirm diagnosis of congenital factor XIII deficiency based on clinical pattern and low FXIII activity; document severity and immediate risk profile.
Explain diagnosis, testing nuance (normal routine coag tests), serious bleeding risk (including intracranial hemorrhage), and emergency plan; begin shared decision-making.
Critical safety step before prescribing any FXIII product or adjuncts; specifically clarify and document penicillin allergy and any prior infusion reactions/blood product reactions.
Non-critical check before prescribing adjuncts (e.g., tranexamic acid) and any concomitant meds; identify additive thrombosis risk or contraindicated combinations.

Initiate hematology-directed FXIII replacement plan (prophylaxis typically ~every 4 weeks; peri-procedural dosing) and/or adjunct tranexamic acid for appropriate bleeding types; provide clear urgent-care triggers.

Address concerns: infusion hypersensitivity signs, generally low thrombosis risk but individualized assessment (esp. with antifibrinolytics), and reinforce that penicillin allergy is not directly related to FXIII products but must be documented.

Lifestyle/safety counseling: avoid high head-trauma risk activities or use protective gear; avoid NSAIDs/aspirin unless approved; acetaminophen preferred; medical alert ID; inform dentists/clinicians; keep emergency/treatment plan accessible.
Arrange hematology follow-up for dosing/monitoring, prophylaxis scheduling, peri-procedural planning, and family screening/genetic counseling as appropriate.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing return precautions, and ensuring follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old woman, vague and a bit evasive. Start with: “I can’t stop peeing and I’m thirsty all the time.” Volunteer only: nonstop thirst/urination, waking 5–6 times nightly, dry mouth despite drinking, carrying a big water bottle/panic without it, daytime fatigue/brain fog. If asked directly, admit you drink several large bottles daily, urine is almost clear, cutting back causes pounding headache, shakiness and feeling sick, occasional lightheadedness standing, and that this began after a hard head injury a few months ago. Mention increased anxiety about bathrooms/water only if probed. Withhold embarrassing details (adult bedwetting, near-accidents, work avoidance) unless the clinician normalizes it and asks gently; then reveal reluctantly. Keep hidden issues (constipation from skipping meals, dry skin/lips, less regular periods/low libido, craving ice water) unless specifically asked. Misconceptions: worry it’s diabetes; also wonder about migraines, stress/anxiety, or sleep apnea. If clinician shows empathy, soften, give slightly more detail, but worry about costs/copays and missing night-shift pay; hint you might stretch expensive meds.

Water deprivation test - urine osmolalit.normal_range: Typically rises >600 mOsm/kg with intact ADH axis

Desmopressin challenge - urine osmolalit.normal_range: Expected significant rise in central DI; minimal change in nephrogenic DI

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
confounders: Primary polydipsia risk (anxiety-related high water intake)
SSRI-associated SIADH risk (could mask hypernatremia if fluid intake changes)
Recent viral gastroenteritis with decreased oral intake (worsens dehydration/hypernatremia)
High caffeine intake (mild diuretic effect contributing to polyuria)

differentials: developmental delay, intellectual disability, obesity, and dysmorphic features

correct_diagnosis.primary: Central diabetes insipidus
acceptable_alternatives: Diabetes insipidus (central) due to hypothalamic-pituitary dysfunction
required_evidence: Polyuria with elevated 24-hour urine volume (typically >3 L/day in adults or >50 mL/kg/day) with inappropriately dilute urine
Low urine osmolality and/or low urine specific gravity despite high/normal serum osmolality
Serum sodium high-normal to elevated in the setting of free-water loss (especially if water access is limited)
Normal fasting serum glucose (to exclude osmotic diuresis from diabetes mellitus)
Renal function not severely impaired (creatinine not markedly elevated) to support a concentrating defect rather than advanced renal failure
Clinical response to desmopressin with increased urine osmolality and decreased urine volume (if a therapeutic trial is used as confirmatory evidence)

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium (Na+): High-normal to elevated (often >145 mmol/L if water intake is inadequate); may be normal if compensatory polydipsia is intact
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: High-normal to elevated (often >295 mOsm/kg) consistent with free-water loss
expected_lab_results.Urine osmolality: Low/inappropriately dilute (often <300 mOsm/kg, frequently <200 mOsm/kg) despite elevated serum osmolality
expected_lab_results.24-hour urine volume: Elevated polyuria (typically >3 L/day in adults; can be markedly higher)
expected_lab_results.Serum glucose (fasting): Normal (to exclude osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia)
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be mildly elevated due to dehydration/volume depletion; can be normal
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal or mildly elevated if dehydrated; not markedly elevated

medications: Desmopressin 0.05 mg PO twice daily (titrate to lowest effective dose; typical range 0.05-0.2 mg PO 2-3 times daily)
OR Desmopressin 10 mcg intranasal once daily to twice daily (titrate; consider if oral not suitable)

If acute symptomatic hypernatremia/dehydration: Dextrose 5% in water (D5W) IV, rate individualized to correct serum sodium slowly (generally <=10-12 mmol/L per 24 hours), with frequent sodium monitoring
non_pharmacological: Ensure free access to water and advise drinking to thirst; avoid deliberate fluid restriction
Daily weights and symptom diary (thirst, nocturia, urine volume) during titration
Monitor serum sodium and osmolality after starting/titrating desmopressin to prevent hyponatremia/water intoxication
Evaluate underlying etiology of central DI (e.g., pituitary/hypothalamic pathology) and assess other pituitary hormone deficiencies as indicated
Educate on sick-day rules: hold/reduce desmopressin and seek care if reduced oral intake, vomiting/diarrhea, confusion, headache, or sudden weight gain
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Within 1 week after initiation or dose change (earlier—24 to 72 hours—if high risk for dysnatremia), then every 3-6 months once stable

must_include: Condition is due to insufficient antidiuretic hormone (ADH/vasopressin) production/release leading to inability to concentrate urine
This causes large volumes of dilute urine and thirst; dehydration and high sodium can occur if water intake cannot keep up
How available labs support the diagnosis (high/normal serum osmolality with inappropriately dilute urine)
Desmopressin replaces ADH and should reduce urine volume and thirst
Use the lowest effective dose; do not take extra doses to 'catch up'
Main risk is hyponatremia/water intoxication; warning signs include headache, nausea, confusion, sudden weight gain, seizures—seek urgent care
Discuss monitoring plan for sodium and symptoms, especially after starting or changing dose
Clarify when to seek urgent care (severe confusion, seizures, inability to keep fluids down, signs of severe dehydration)
If developmental delay/intellectual disability is present, confirm caregiver understanding and establish a safe medication/fluids supervision plan
Maintain access to water and drink to thirst; avoid fluid restriction unless specifically instructed
Track daily weight and urine output if feasible during titration
Sick-day guidance: if significantly reduced intake or vomiting/diarrhea, hold/reduce desmopressin and contact clinician for sodium check

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for polyuria/polydipsia, duration, nocturia, dehydration symptoms, neuro/endocrine history (head trauma, pituitary surgery/tumor), pregnancy/postpartum, medication causes (lithium, demeclocycline), fluid access, and red flags (confusion, syncope).

Obtain diagnostic labs to confirm hypotonic polyuria and assess severity: serum sodium, serum osmolality, glucose, BUN/Cr, urine osmolality, urine specific gravity, urine volume (24h or estimated), and consider calcium/potassium to exclude other causes.
Interpret labs for DI pattern: high/normal serum osmolality and/or hypernatremia with inappropriately dilute urine (low urine osmolality/specific gravity).

Make diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus when hypotonic polyuria is present with inappropriately dilute urine and clinical context supports ADH deficiency; ensure competing diagnoses are addressed (primary polydipsia, osmotic diuresis, nephrogenic DI).
Critical safety check before desmopressin or any other prescribing.
Non-critical safety check for medications that increase hyponatremia risk or affect water balance (e.g., SSRIs, carbamazepine, thiazides, NSAIDs) before prescribing desmopressin.
Initiate desmopressin at the lowest effective dose with clear dosing instructions and avoidance of extra doses; include a monitoring plan for sodium and symptom response.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment including hyponatremia risk and warning signs, address concerns/monitoring/urgent care triggers, and lifestyle/sick-day guidance.
Arrange follow-up and lab monitoring (serum sodium) soon after initiation or dose changes; ensure supervision plan if cognitive impairment/caregiver involvement is relevant.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history for polyuria/polydipsia, duration, nocturia, dehydration symptoms, neuro/endocrine history (head trauma, pituitary surgery/tumor), pregnancy/postpartum, medication causes (lithium, demeclocycline), fluid access, and red flags (confusion, syncope).

Obtain diagnostic labs to confirm hypotonic polyuria and assess severity: serum sodium, serum osmolality, glucose, BUN/Cr, urine osmolality, urine specific gravity, urine volume (24h or estimated), and consider calcium/potassium to exclude other causes.
Interpret labs for DI pattern: high/normal serum osmolality and/or hypernatremia with inappropriately dilute urine (low urine osmolality/specific gravity).

Make diagnosis of central diabetes insipidus when hypotonic polyuria is present with inappropriately dilute urine and clinical context supports ADH deficiency; ensure competing diagnoses are addressed (primary polydipsia, osmotic diuresis, nephrogenic DI).
Critical safety check before desmopressin or any other prescribing.
Non-critical safety check for medications that increase hyponatremia risk or affect water balance (e.g., SSRIs, carbamazepine, thiazides, NSAIDs) before prescribing desmopressin.
Initiate desmopressin at the lowest effective dose with clear dosing instructions and avoidance of extra doses; include a monitoring plan for sodium and symptom response.

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment including hyponatremia risk and warning signs, address concerns/monitoring/urgent care triggers, and lifestyle/sick-day guidance.
Arrange follow-up and lab monitoring (serum sodium) soon after initiation or dose changes; ensure supervision plan if cognitive impairment/caregiver involvement is relevant.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

nl_assertions: Agent differentiates central DI from primary polydipsia, osmotic diuresis (e.g., uncontrolled diabetes mellitus), and nephrogenic DI using history and labs (serum osmolality/sodium paired with urine osmolality/specific gravity).

Agent explicitly states that central DI is due to insufficient ADH (vasopressin) production/release causing inability to concentrate urine, leading to large volumes of dilute urine and thirst; notes dehydration and hypernatremia risk if intake cannot keep up.
Agent references available labs to support diagnosis: high/normal serum osmolality and/or hypernatremia with inappropriately dilute urine (low urine osmolality/specific gravity).
Before prescribing, agent performs allergy_check (critical) and does not prescribe if allergy information is unknown or indicates a relevant allergy without addressing it.

Before prescribing, agent performs contraindication_check (critical) and does not prescribe desmopressin in the setting of current/persistent hyponatremia or inability to safely regulate fluid intake without a mitigation plan; agent addresses renal impairment or other relevant contraindications per scenario data if present.
Agent performs drug_interaction_check (non-critical) and, if interacting agents are present, explains increased hyponatremia risk and adjusts monitoring/counseling accordingly.
Agent prescribes desmopressin with lowest-effective-dose intent and clear instruction not to take extra doses to 'catch up' after missed doses.

Agent emphasizes primary treatment risk: hyponatremia/water intoxication; lists warning signs (headache, nausea, confusion, sudden weight gain, seizures) and instructs to seek urgent care if they occur.
Agent provides a concrete monitoring plan for sodium and symptoms after starting or changing dose (timing and triggers for earlier checks).
Agent provides safety-net instructions for urgent care: severe confusion, seizures, inability to keep fluids down, or signs of severe dehydration.
Agent gives lifestyle guidance: maintain access to water and drink to thirst; avoid fluid restriction unless specifically instructed; track daily weight and urine output during titration if feasible.
Agent provides sick-day guidance: if significantly reduced intake or vomiting/diarrhea, hold/reduce desmopressin and contact clinician for sodium check to prevent hyponatremia.

If developmental delay/intellectual disability/caregiver dependence is present in the case data, agent confirms caregiver understanding and establishes a safe supervision plan for medication and fluids.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 22-year-old female college-educated elementary school teaching assistant. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start with: “I keep getting these big bruises and my nose won’t stop bleeding sometimes.” Early in the visit, volunteer: big bruises without remembering injury, long nosebleeds, gum bleeding with brushing, and feeling more tired than usual.

If asked about bleeding history, give details: small cuts ooze долго, heavy bleeding after dental cleanings/tooth pull, heavy periods soaking pads/tampons quickly, deep painful swelling after bumps, and one urgent care visit for bleeding that wouldn’t stop.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: a couple episodes of very dark stools, more shortness of breath on stairs, craving ice, and avoiding activities due to fear of bruising.

Be reluctant/embarrassed and only disclose if directly asked in a nonjudgmental way: bleeding after sex and rectal bleeding after constipation/straining.

Express misconceptions: wonder if frequent ibuprofen or a “natural circulation” supplement is causing it; mention “thin blood/low platelets.” Voice cost/job/insurance worries and desire to skip follow-up labs. If the clinician shows empathy, you relax, thank them, and share more details.

Desmopressin (DDAVP) 0.3 mcg/kg IV once prior to minor procedures (per hematology plan)
confounders: Recent use of NSAIDs for dysmenorrhea (worsens mucosal bleeding and can mimic a platelet function disorder)
Heavy menstrual bleeding causing iron deficiency anemia, which may be misattributed as the primary problem
Possible recent initiation of estrogen-containing oral contraceptive for cycle control (can raise factor VIII levels and partially mask severity on repeat testing)
Family history negative for hemophilia (autosomal recessive condition may be overlooked in females)

acceptable_alternatives: Combined factor V and factor VIII deficiency

required_evidence: Bleeding tendency (e.g., easy bruising, epistaxis, mucosal bleeding, menorrhagia, excessive bleeding after procedures) with otherwise normal platelet count
Simultaneous prolongation of PT/INR and aPTT on coagulation studies

History suggestive of inherited bleeding disorder and/or similar family history; symptoms often mild-to-moderate
Exclusion of common acquired causes of prolonged PT and aPTT (e.g., anticoagulant use, severe liver disease, disseminated intravascular coagulation) based on history/exam and available labs

Tranexamic acid 1 g PO every 8 hours for 3-5 days for mucosal bleeding (e.g., epistaxis, dental bleeding) if no contraindications

Local hemostatic measures for epistaxis/mucosal bleeding (direct pressure, topical agents, nasal packing if needed)
Hematology consultation for confirmation (factor assays and mixing study when available) and individualized peri-procedural plan
Provide/encourage medical alert identification indicating combined factor V and VIII deficiency and NSAID bleeding risk
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Hematology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (sooner: within 24-72 hours if active bleeding, upcoming procedure, or abnormal/unstable labs)

must_include: This is an inherited bleeding disorder where two clotting factors (V and VIII) are low, leading to slower clot formation
Why PT and aPTT are both prolonged and why platelet count is usually normal
Bleeding is often mild-to-moderate but can worsen with surgery/dental work/trauma
Treatment depends on severity and situation (active bleeding vs procedure prevention)
FFP helps replace factor V; factor VIII concentrate replaces factor VIII; DDAVP may help some patients for mild cases
When to seek urgent care (uncontrolled bleeding, head injury, black/tarry stools, heavy/prolonged menstrual bleeding, large expanding bruises)
Discuss medication avoidance: NSAIDs (especially ibuprofen) can worsen bleeding; use safer alternatives for pain/fever as advised (e.g., acetaminophen within recommended dosing)
Clarify that anticoagulants/aspirin should not be started without clinician review
Explain expected monitoring and need for hematology planning before procedures

Avoid high-risk trauma activities when possible and use protective measures

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit bleeding history, triggers (surgery/dental work/trauma), current bleeding severity, meds (NSAIDs/aspirin/anticoagulants), family history, and prior lab/diagnosis to gauge urgency and guide testing.

Interpret results for the benchmark: both PT and aPTT prolonged with normal platelet count; low factor V and low factor VIII levels consistent with combined deficiency (often with correction on mixing if congenital).
State the diagnosis (combined deficiency of factor V and factor VIII) and differentiate from hemophilia A alone (isolated aPTT) and platelet disorders (low platelets/abnormal function).

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (inheritance, PT/aPTT rationale, typical bleeding pattern), explain treatment options (FFP for factor V, factor VIII concentrate, DDAVP for some mild cases, procedure prophylaxis vs active bleed), urgent return precautions, and medication avoidance (NSAIDs/aspirin/anticoagulants without review), plus planning with hematology.
Arrange hematology follow-up for individualized bleeding plan, peri-procedural planning, and discussion of family implications/genetic counseling/testing; provide written emergency plan if available.
Close encounter after ensuring patient understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

patient.instructions: You are ZhangXX, a 46-year-old male IT project manager. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Open with: “I keep getting this tight, heavy feeling in my chest when I walk up stairs.” Early in the visit, volunteer that the pressure is centered in the chest, triggered by exertion (stairs/carrying groceries), improves after a few minutes of rest, and you get more short of breath with fast walking or hills. correct_diagnosis.primary: Congenital myasthenic syndrome (CMS) due to glycosylation pathway defect (e.g., GMPPB-related CMS)
required_evidence: Chronic, fatigable muscle weakness with fluctuating severity (often ocular/bulbar/limb-girdle) beginning in childhood or early life
Clinical history consistent with congenital (non-autoimmune) neuromuscular junction disorder (no typical autoimmune MG triggers; often long-standing course)
Worsening weakness/respiratory symptoms with macrolides (e.g., azithromycin), consistent with neuromuscular junction transmission vulnerability
Laboratory pattern not suggesting primary inflammatory myopathy (CK normal to mildly elevated; transaminases may be normal or mildly elevated from muscle source)
Exclusion of common mimics by available labs (normal thyroid function and B12; electrolytes not markedly deranged)
Known or suspected genetic diagnosis of glycosylation-defect CMS (if available in case data) or phenotype compatible with glycosylation-defect CMS

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to mildly elevated (may be persistently mildly elevated in some glycosylation-defect CMS such as GMPPB/GFPT1/DPAGT1)
expected_lab_results.Aspartate aminotransferase (AST): Normal or mildly elevated (can be muscle-derived if CK elevated)
expected_lab_results.Alanine aminotransferase (ALT): Normal or mildly elevated (less commonly muscle-derived than AST, but may be slightly high if muscle injury present)
expected_lab_results.25-hydroxy Vitamin D: Often low or insufficient (common; not causal but can worsen myalgias/fatigue)

medications: Pyridostigmine 30 mg PO every 6 hours while awake (titrate to effect and tolerability; typical range 30–60 mg per dose)
3,4-diaminopyridine (amifampridine) 10 mg PO three times daily (titrate slowly; do not exceed 80 mg/day; specialist-directed)
Albuterol (salbutamol) 2 mg PO three times daily (or equivalent long-acting beta-2 agonist regimen) if inadequate response or subtype suggests benefit; monitor heart rate/tremor
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000–2000 IU PO daily if 25-OH vitamin D low/insufficient
non_pharmacological: Avoid medications known to worsen neuromuscular junction transmission (especially macrolides/azithromycin; also use caution with fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, magnesium, beta-blockers, and sedatives when possible)
Respiratory risk plan: educate on signs of impending respiratory failure (increasing dyspnea, weak cough, inability to count to 20 in one breath) and when to seek emergency care
Physical therapy/graded activity with pacing and energy conservation; avoid overexertion that provokes prolonged weakness
Swallow safety evaluation if bulbar symptoms present; aspiration precautions as indicated
Genetic counseling and referral to neuromuscular specialist for subtype confirmation and therapy optimization
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular follow-up in 2–4 weeks after medication initiation/titration (earlier within 1 week if respiratory/bulbar symptoms or adverse effects), then every 3–6 months once stable

must_include: Condition affects the neuromuscular junction and is typically genetic (congenital), not the autoimmune form of myasthenia gravis
Symptoms fluctuate and worsen with fatigue/illness and certain medications
Why routine labs may be normal or only mildly abnormal and do not rule the condition in/out
Goal is to improve neuromuscular transmission and reduce fatigable weakness; response can vary by genetic subtype

Arrange follow-up to assess response, side effects, and functional outcomes; include earlier review if bulbar/respiratory symptoms, and plan for neuromuscular specialist/genetics referral if not already established.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 34-year-old mother and part-time administrative assistant. You are cooperative and speak clearly, but you’re worried and tired. Start by saying your daughter has been losing skills she used to have and keeps doing repetitive hand movements. Early in the visit, volunteer: she used to say a few words but now barely talks and mostly makes sounds; she constantly wrings/rubs her hands; she no longer uses her hands to play like before; she’s unsteady, falls more; sleep is very broken with frequent night waking.

If asked, share: she developed normally at first then regressed around toddler age; unexplained anxiety/long crying spells; breath-holding or fast breathing bursts when excited; brief staring spells with unresponsiveness; constipation; nighttime teeth grinding.

Do not mention unless directly asked: cold hands/feet, low hunger/thirst cues, coughing with thin liquids, mild spine curvature.

Only reveal later if asked about toileting: occasional daytime wetting.

If the clinician suggests genetics/specialists, express misconceptions: you assume insurance will deny testing and follow-ups; you think therapies are all out-of-pocket; you fear disability services could harm employment or credit. If met with empathy, soften, thank them, and ask for practical help/resources.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Atypical Rett syndrome
required_evidence: Neurodevelopmental regression after a period of apparently typical early development
Loss of purposeful hand skills with development of stereotyped hand movements (e.g., wringing/washing)
Severe impairment of expressive language and gait abnormalities
Supportive features may include seizures, breathing irregularities, scoliosis, growth deceleration/microcephaly
No clinical syndrome consistent with acute infectious encephalitis (e.g., Japanese encephalitis) such as acute febrile illness with altered mental status

expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (WBC): Typically normal (no leukocytosis expected in atypical Rett syndrome)
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (Hemoglobin): May be low if concomitant iron deficiency; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Complete blood count (MCV): Low if iron deficiency present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Ferritin: Low if iron deficiency present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Serum iron: Low if iron deficiency present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.TIBC: High if iron deficiency present; otherwise normal
expected_lab_results.Transferrin saturation: Low if iron deficiency present; otherwise normal

medications: Ferrous sulfate 3 mg/kg/day elemental iron PO once daily (if iron deficiency confirmed by low ferritin and low transferrin saturation)
non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis with genetics referral/testing (MECP2 sequencing; consider CDKL5/FOXG1 if MECP2 negative and phenotype suggests)
Multidisciplinary supportive care: PT/OT, speech/communication therapy (AAC), nutritional assessment and feeding support
Screen and manage common complications: seizures (neurology), scoliosis (orthopedics), breathing dysrhythmia, constipation, sleep problems
Developmental services/IEP and caregiver support resources
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-8 weeks; if on iron therapy, repeat hemoglobin and iron studies in 4-8 weeks and continue iron for ~3 months after normalization

must_include: Atypical Rett syndrome is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by regression and specific movement/communication features
This is not an acute brain infection like Japanese encephalitis (which typically presents with fever and acute altered mental status)
Genetic testing helps confirm the diagnosis and guides counseling
No curative medication; care focuses on symptom management and maximizing function/quality of life
If iron deficiency is present, iron supplementation is recommended and labs will be rechecked
Importance of multidisciplinary therapies (PT/OT/speech, nutrition) and monitoring for seizures/scoliosis
Discuss prognosis variability and expected course, emphasizing supportive interventions
Review red flags that would warrant urgent evaluation (new fever, acute change in alertness, new focal deficits, dehydration/poor intake)
Offer caregiver support resources and discuss genetic counseling for family planning
Nutrition and constipation prevention strategies (hydration, fiber as appropriate, bowel regimen if needed)
Safe feeding guidance and aspiration precautions if swallowing issues are present
Encourage routine physical activity/positioning to reduce contractures and support mobility

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX’s mother bringing your 3-year-old daughter for “another sudden high fever.” Act anxious and speak vaguely ("she just crashes"). Start by volunteering: fevers come on fast, get very high, last a few days; she’s very tired/weak, eats poorly, sometimes vomits; she gets shaky/sweaty if she hasn’t eaten because she has diabetes. Mention you think it’s "just the flu" or a stomach bug, maybe an allergy because of rash, or teething/ear infections; also worry about UTI because she sometimes says it burns to pee.

Only if asked, add: blotchy red rash sometimes, belly pain/loose stools, mouth sores, joint aches (knees/ankles), more seizures with fever, and a past urine test with “high mevalonic acid.”

Do not volunteer at first: irritability the day before, triggers after shots/colds/stress, slow growth, developmental delays/small head, fruity breath/high sugars, dehydration/less peeing.

Resist/hesitate to share: sores around her bottom, missed insulin doses (guilt), fear it’s inherited and you’ll be blamed.

If the clinician is empathetic, you soften, share more, and ask about costs/insurance, travel, and missing work.

expected_lab_results.Urine mevalonic acid: Markedly elevated (diagnostic; may be highest during febrile/inflammatory episodes)

expected_lab_results.Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR): Often elevated during attacks; may be normal between episodes

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: May be low-normal or mildly decreased if chronic inflammation; otherwise can be normal

medications: Canakinumab 4 mg/kg subcutaneous every 4 weeks (max 300 mg) for recurrent inflammatory episodes

non_pharmacological: Urgent metabolic-genetics referral for confirmatory testing/management and family counseling
Sick-day plan: early evaluation with fever, poor intake, vomiting, lethargy; maintain hydration and caloric intake; low threshold for hospital assessment
Avoid known triggers when possible (intercurrent infections, prolonged fasting); ensure routine vaccinations and prompt infection management
Monitor for complications of chronic inflammation (growth delay, anemia, amyloidosis) and treatment adverse effects
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess within 2–4 weeks after initiating/adjusting anti-inflammatory therapy; then every 3 months with symptom review and CRP/ESR/CBC monitoring (or sooner for flares).

If asked about development/history, share: floppy baby with feeding trouble, learning/planning difficulties, shorter than family, never feel full, high pain tolerance, skin/scab picking, loud snoring with occasional gasping, constipation. Do not mention unless directly asked: rarely thirsty/low drinking, low sweating/temperature issues, daytime sleepiness despite sleep, slow-healing sores/infections. Only if strong rapport or direct questioning, admit with embarrassment: sneaking/secret eating, delayed sexual development/low libido, occasional adult bedwetting when very tired. Express misconceptions: worry about thyroid, Cushing’s (bruising/stretch marks), and frustration with being told it’s “just binge eating” or willpower. Emphasize money concerns about coverage and cost of healthy foods. If the clinician is empathic, you relax, answer more fully, and say you want help without being judged.

Genetic testing - MAGEL2 sequencing.value: Pathogenic heterozygous truncating variant detected (consistent with Schaaf-Yang syndrome / Prader-Willi-like phenotype)

medications: Metformin ER 500 mg PO once daily with evening meal
Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 2000 IU PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily
comorbidities: Suspected nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (mild transaminitis)
confounders: Binge-eating disorder vs genetic hyperphagia (overlapping behavioral presentation)
Atypical depression/anxiety contributing to weight gain and low motivation
Sedative sensitivity and possible sleep-disordered breathing (obesity-related) affecting daytime somnolence and activity
History of developmental delay/autism-spectrum traits (common in MAGEL2-related disorder) complicating behavioral assessment and adherence
Normal 15q11-q13 methylation testing can mislead away from PWS despite Prader-Willi-like phenotype

Severe obesity with insulin resistance in a genetic hyperphagia syndrome
required_evidence: Known pathogenic point mutation causing Prader-Willi-like phenotype (documented genetic testing)
Clinical phenotype consistent with Prader-Willi spectrum: hyperphagia/food-seeking, early-onset obesity, hypotonia/developmental delay and/or characteristic behavioral features
Metabolic risk assessment showing insulin resistance and/or prediabetes/diabetes risk (elevated fasting insulin and/or elevated fasting glucose/A1c) and/or dyslipidemia
Exclusion/low suspicion of alternative endocrine causes of obesity (e.g., Cushing syndrome, untreated hypothyroidism) based on history/physical and prior workup if available

expected_lab_results.Fasting glucose: Normal to mildly elevated (impaired fasting glucose possible in obesity/insulin resistance)
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Normal to mildly elevated (prediabetes range possible; diabetes if significantly elevated)
expected_lab_results.Lipid panel - Total cholesterol: Normal to elevated; often elevated with obesity-related dyslipidemia

medications: Metformin 500 mg PO once daily with food for 1 week, then 500 mg PO twice daily as tolerated (for insulin resistance/prediabetes if present)
Semaglutide 0.25 mg SC weekly for 4 weeks, then 0.5 mg SC weekly; titrate per response/tolerability (for obesity with hyperphagia; if age-appropriate and no contraindications)
non_pharmacological: Structured food security plan: locked/controlled access to food, consistent meal schedule, portion control, caregiver-supervised environment

Physical activity plan tailored to ability: goal ≥150 min/week moderate activity (or pediatric equivalent) plus strength training 2 days/week; incorporate daily routine
Behavioral/psychology support for hyperphagia-related behaviors and family coping strategies
Screen and manage obesity comorbidities: sleep apnea evaluation if symptoms; NAFLD risk counseling; blood pressure monitoring
Consider endocrinology/genetics follow-up for syndrome-specific management (e.g., growth hormone therapy evaluation if appropriate and already not addressed)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks for medication tolerability/weight trajectory; repeat metabolic labs (A1c, fasting glucose, lipids, ALT) in 3 months or sooner if abnormal

must_include: This is a genetic syndrome that affects appetite regulation and metabolism, leading to hyperphagia and high risk of obesity-related complications
The point mutation causes a Prader-Willi-like presentation; management focuses on safety, structured environment, and preventing complications
Metabolic risks include insulin resistance, prediabetes/diabetes, dyslipidemia, and fatty liver
Food security and structured supervision are core treatments and often more important than willpower-based strategies
Medication goals: improve insulin resistance and reduce appetite/weight-related complications; review expected benefits and realistic timelines
Review common side effects and what to do: metformin GI upset; GLP-1 RA nausea/vomiting/constipation, dehydration risk; when to seek care (persistent vomiting, severe abdominal pain)

Discuss contraindications and safety screening for GLP-1 RA (history of medullary thyroid carcinoma/MEN2, pancreatitis) and pregnancy considerations if relevant
Shared decision-making with caregiver/patient about medication choice, injection training if applicable, and cost/coverage barriers
Provide specific, actionable plan for meals/snacks, food access control, and caregiver consistency
Encourage regular physical activity matched to functional level; reduce sedentary time
Plan for monitoring: weight/waist trends, symptoms of dysglycemia, and scheduled lab follow-up

profile.occupation: elementary school teacher

patient.chief_complaint: My eyesight has been slowly getting worse over the last few months.

volunteer: My eyesight has been slowly getting worse over the last few months.
I keep bumping into things on the sides, like I don’t notice people or objects until they’re right next to me.
I’ve been getting more headaches behind my eyes, especially later in the day.
I feel more tired than usual even when I sleep enough.
if_asked: Bright lights bother me more than they used to.
I’ve had some nausea with the headaches, but I don’t usually throw up.
I’ve been feeling colder than other people lately.
I’ve gained a bit of weight without changing what I eat.
My skin feels drier than usual and I’m more constipated.
I get lightheaded when I stand up quickly.
I’ve noticed I’m not as strong during workouts and I recover slower.
My periods have become irregular and lighter than they used to be.
My sex drive has been lower.
hidden: I’ve had trouble concentrating and I feel kind of ‘slowed down’ mentally.
My hair seems to be thinning a bit and my eyebrows look sparser.
I’m not sweating as much as I used to, even when it’s warm.
I’ve been drinking more water and peeing more often than before, including waking up at night to pee.
resistant: I’ve had some milky discharge from my nipples even though I’m not pregnant or breastfeeding.
misleading: I thought it was just eye strain from screens, so I tried blue-light glasses and breaks but it didn’t really help.
I wondered if it was migraines because of the headaches and light sensitivity, but the vision problem feels more constant than a typical migraine.
I’ve been stressed at work and figured the tiredness was burnout.
noise: My seasonal allergies have been acting up with a stuffy nose and sneezing.
I’ve had some mild jaw clicking when I chew.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked whether the vision change affects side vision or driving/walking.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked about temperature intolerance and other body changes along with fatigue/weight gain.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked specifically about menstrual changes and reproductive symptoms.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Revealed when asked directly about thirst, urination frequency, and nighttime urination.

progressive_reveal.trigger: Only disclosed after a nonjudgmental, specific question about nipple discharge and reassurance about privacy/normalization.

general_concerns: I thought my worsening vision is probably just eye strain from screens or needing a stronger glasses prescription, not something in the brain.
If it’s a cyst, I assume it’s cancer or will inevitably turn into cancer.
I’m worried that MRI radiation could damage my body or fertility.
If my hormones are low, I assume taking hormones will permanently shut down my body’s ability to make them and make me dependent forever.
I think headaches and vision changes mean I should avoid exercise and normal activity so the cyst doesn’t ‘burst’ or grow faster.
economic_concerns: I’m afraid the MRI and specialist visits won’t be covered and I’ll get a large surprise bill.
I assume hormone replacements are always expensive brand-name medications and I won’t be able to afford them long term.
I’m worried that needing follow-up scans and labs will mean repeated time off work and lost income.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 34-year-old elementary school teacher with college education. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by volunteering: your eyesight has slowly worsened over months; you bump into things on the sides; you get headaches behind your eyes later in the day; you feel unusually tired despite enough sleep. If asked, add: bright lights bother you; headaches sometimes include nausea; you feel colder, have mild weight gain, dry skin, constipation, lightheadedness on standing, reduced workout strength/recovery, irregular lighter periods, and lower sex drive. Do not mention unless directly asked: trouble concentrating/feeling slowed down, thinning hair/sparser eyebrows, reduced sweating, increased thirst and urination (including nighttime). If asked specifically about nipple/breast discharge, be hesitant and a bit embarrassed; only then reveal milky nipple discharge and say you weren’t sure it mattered. Express misconceptions: you tried blue-light glasses/breaks for screen strain; wondered about migraines; blamed fatigue on work stress/burnout. If the clinician shows empathy, acknowledge it and become more open. Bring up cost worries about MRI, specialists, and long-term hormone meds, and concern about missing work for follow-ups.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ethinyl estradiol/levonorgestrel 30 mcg/150 mcg PO daily (combined oral contraceptive)
confounders: Combined oral contraceptive use (can mask hypogonadism/amenorrhea and alters gonadotropin/estradiol interpretation)
Sertraline use and depression-related fatigue (overlaps with hypothyroid/adrenal insufficiency symptoms)
Mild anemia contributing to fatigue and decreased exercise tolerance
Mild hyperprolactinemia from stalk effect can mimic prolactinoma and contribute to menstrual irregularity

diagnosis.primary: pituitary deficiency due to Rathke's pouch cysts

correct_diagnosis.primary: Hypopituitarism due to Rathke’s cleft cyst with optic chiasm compression (visual decline) and secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism
acceptable_alternatives: Pituitary stalk/parasellar mass (Rathke’s cleft cyst vs nonfunctioning pituitary adenoma) with hypopituitarism and chiasmal compression
Central adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism due to pituitary lesion
required_evidence: Progressive visual acuity decline consistent with sellar/suprasellar mass effect on optic apparatus
Low 08:00 serum cortisol with inappropriately low/normal plasma ACTH (secondary adrenal insufficiency pattern)
Low free T4 with inappropriately low/normal TSH (central hypothyroidism pattern)
Prolactin normal or mildly elevated (stalk effect possible)
No primary electrolyte pattern required, but hyponatremia may occur in secondary adrenal insufficiency

expected_lab_results.Serum sodium: Often low or low-normal (hyponatremia can occur with secondary adrenal insufficiency); potassium typically normal
expected_lab_results.Serum potassium: Normal (helps distinguish secondary from primary adrenal insufficiency, where hyperkalemia is expected)
expected_lab_results.Serum osmolality: Low or low-normal if hyponatremic (hypotonic hyponatremia possible)
expected_lab_results.Morning serum cortisol (08:00): Low (e.g., <3–5 µg/dL strongly suggestive) or inappropriately low for illness/stress
expected_lab_results.Plasma ACTH: Low or inappropriately normal (consistent with secondary adrenal insufficiency)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Low or inappropriately normal (not appropriately elevated despite low FT4)
expected_lab_results.Prolactin: Normal to mildly elevated (stalk effect possible; not markedly elevated as in prolactinoma)

medications: Hydrocortisone 15 mg PO each morning and 5 mg PO each afternoon
Hydrocortisone 100 mg IM as emergency injection PRN suspected adrenal crisis (with patient training and kit)
Levothyroxine 50 mcg PO once daily (start only after glucocorticoid replacement is initiated; dose adjust by free T4)
non_pharmacological: Urgent MRI pituitary with contrast and formal visual field testing (perimetry) for optic pathway compression
Urgent endocrinology referral for full pituitary axis assessment and dynamic testing as indicated
Neurosurgery referral for symptomatic Rathke’s cleft cyst with visual symptoms (consider transsphenoidal decompression/drainage)
Adrenal insufficiency education: sick-day rules (double/triple oral hydrocortisone during febrile illness), medical alert identification, carry steroid card
Advise patient to seek emergency care for vomiting/inability to take PO steroids, severe weakness, syncope, or altered mental status
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Endocrinology within 1–2 weeks; neurosurgery/ophthalmology urgently (within days) given progressive visual symptoms; recheck free T4 in 6 weeks after levothyroxine initiation/titration and clinical review of steroid adequacy within 2–4 weeks

must_include: A Rathke’s cleft cyst is a benign pituitary-region cyst that can compress the optic chiasm and reduce pituitary hormone production
Visual changes are a warning sign of pressure on the optic pathways and require urgent imaging and eye testing
Blood tests suggest the pituitary is not signaling the adrenal and thyroid glands properly (secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism)
Start cortisol replacement first; thyroid hormone is started only after steroid therapy to avoid precipitating adrenal crisis
Hydrocortisone daily schedule and when to increase doses during illness (sick-day rules)
Emergency hydrocortisone injection indications and when to call emergency services
Levothyroxine dosing, adherence, and monitoring using free T4 (TSH is unreliable in central hypothyroidism)
Need for specialist evaluation and possible surgery to protect vision and relieve compression
Discuss potential side effects of glucocorticoids (weight gain, mood changes, glucose effects) and how physiologic replacement differs from high-dose steroids
Discuss levothyroxine side effects and symptoms of over/under-replacement
Explain that penicillin allergy is noted and will be avoided; current planned medications are not penicillins
Provide clear red flags: worsening vision, severe headache, vomiting, fainting, confusion
Wear medical alert identification and carry a steroid emergency card
Keep an emergency injection kit accessible and ensure family/partner knows how to use it
Do not stop hydrocortisone abruptly; ensure adequate supply and plan for travel
Follow-up schedule and importance of monitoring symptoms and labs

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key symptoms and risks: visual changes (bitemporal field loss, blurred vision), headache, fatigue, weight change, dizziness/syncope, nausea/vomiting, salt craving, menstrual/sexual dysfunction, polyuria/polydipsia; screen for acute adrenal crisis and pituitary apoplexy red flags; ask pregnancy status and current meds.

Order baseline pituitary/adrenal/thyroid evaluation to confirm secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism and assess other pituitary axes: 8am cortisol, ACTH, free T4, TSH, prolactin, IGF-1, LH/FSH with estradiol/testosterone as appropriate, CMP (Na/K/glucose), CBC; consider serum osmolality/urine osmolality if DI symptoms.

Interpret labs for hypopituitarism pattern (low cortisol with low/inappropriately normal ACTH; low free T4 with low/inappropriately normal TSH; possible other axis deficiencies; hyponatremia/hypoglycemia).

Synthesize into pituitary deficiency likely due to Rathke’s cleft cyst with secondary adrenal insufficiency and central hypothyroidism; recognize urgency if visual symptoms/headache suggest compression or apoplexy and need for urgent imaging/ophthalmology/neurosurgery.
Required critical safety check before prescribing; confirm reported penicillin allergy and reactions; document and ensure planned meds are not penicillins.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review medication list for interactions affecting levothyroxine absorption (iron, calcium, PPIs) and glucocorticoid metabolism (CYP inducers/inhibitors).

Initiate physiologic glucocorticoid replacement first (e.g., hydrocortisone divided dosing) with sick-day rules and emergency injection plan; only then prescribe levothyroxine with dosing and monitoring by free T4 (not TSH).

Deliver required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, urgent visual warning signs, treatment sequencing (steroids before thyroid), sick-day rules, emergency injection, side effects, red flags, medical alert and emergency card, adherence and monitoring.

Arrange urgent ophthalmology/visual field testing and pituitary MRI if not already done; endocrinology and neurosurgery referral; set lab monitoring timeline (free T4 after dose changes; electrolytes/glucose; symptom review) and safety-net instructions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, providing written plan, and ensuring emergency instructions and follow-up are set.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Intellectual developmental disorder
acceptable_alternatives: Intellectual developmental disorder, autosomal dominant 63, with macrocephaly
required_evidence: History of onset during the developmental period with persistent deficits in intellectual functioning (learning, reasoning, problem-solving) impacting daily life
Deficits in adaptive functioning across conceptual, social, and/or practical domains requiring ongoing support
Symptoms not better explained by untreated hypothyroidism, vitamin B12 deficiency, or other reversible metabolic causes (screening labs reviewed)
If considering syndromic/genetic differential: macrocephaly and/or dysmorphic features, positive family history, or associated neurodevelopmental/behavioral phenotype prompting genetics referral

expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (no evidence of untreated hypothyroidism contributing to cognitive impairment)
expected_lab_results.Vitamin B12: Within reference range (no B12-deficiency-related cognitive impairment)
expected_lab_results.25-OH Vitamin D: Often low/insufficient; if low, treat for bone/overall health but not causative for IDD
expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin A1c: Within reference range (no uncontrolled diabetes contributing to cognition)
expected_lab_results.Lipid panel - Total cholesterol: May be normal or mildly elevated depending on diet/activity; not diagnostic
expected_lab_results.Lipid panel - LDL-C: May be normal or mildly elevated; manage per age/risk if elevated

medications: Cholecalciferol (vitamin D3) 1000-2000 IU PO daily
non_pharmacological: Confirm severity and functional needs with standardized assessment (e.g., IQ testing plus adaptive functioning scales) and review school/early records
Refer to developmental-behavioral pediatrics or adult neurodevelopmental specialist (age-appropriate) for comprehensive care planning

Refer to clinical genetics for evaluation of syndromic causes (including Turnpenny-Fry syndrome and AD IDD 63 with macrocephaly) and counseling; consider chromosomal microarray and/or exome sequencing per genetics
Initiate/continue individualized educational/vocational supports (IEP/504 if applicable), speech-language therapy, occupational therapy, and behavioral therapy as indicated by deficits
Screen and manage common comorbidities: ADHD, autism spectrum disorder, anxiety/depression, sleep disorders, seizures, hearing/vision problems; refer as indicated
Caregiver education and connection to community resources (case management, disability services, respite care, social work)
Lifestyle counseling: regular physical activity, balanced diet, sleep routine; address cardiovascular risk if lipids elevated
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: 4-8 weeks to review lab results, functional assessments, and referrals; then every 3-6 months to monitor supports, comorbidities, and safety

must_include: Definition: lifelong neurodevelopmental condition with onset in childhood involving intellectual and adaptive functioning limitations
Diagnosis is based on functional assessment and history, not solely on a single lab test
Discuss possibility of an underlying genetic syndrome if features such as macrocephaly/family history are present and why genetics referral is helpful
No medication treats IDD itself; management focuses on supports, therapies, and treating comorbid conditions
Outline referrals (specialist and genetics) and what each will evaluate
If vitamin D is low: explain supplementation purpose and expected duration
Elicit patient/caregiver goals and main challenges (school/work, daily living, behavior, safety)
Discuss prognosis in terms of maximizing function and independence with tailored supports
Review red flags needing urgent care (new regression, seizures, severe behavioral change, safety risks)
Sleep hygiene and routine
Nutrition and physical activity; address lipid/glucose abnormalities if present
Community resources/disability services and caregiver support options

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit developmental history, adaptive functioning, school/work performance, supports, caregiver concerns/goals, and screen for red flags (regression, seizures, safety risks) and comorbidities (ADHD, ASD, anxiety, sleep issues).

Order basic labs to assess common reversible contributors/comorbidities and baseline health (e.g., CBC, CMP, TSH, B12/folate, vitamin D, fasting lipids, glucose/A1c) and consider targeted genetic testing/referral triggers.
Review results to guide counseling (e.g., vitamin D deficiency, lipid/glucose abnormalities) and determine if additional workup/referrals are indicated.

Make/confirm IDD diagnosis based on history and functional assessment (intellectual + adaptive functioning limitations with childhood onset), not a single lab test; document severity/needs where possible.

Explain diagnosis, prognosis, and management plan emphasizing supports/therapies and treatment of comorbidities; discuss genetics referral if indicated; provide lifestyle guidance and community resources.
Critical safety check before any prescribing (e.g., vitamin D or other meds for comorbid conditions).
Non-critical safety check before prescribing to avoid drug-drug interactions, especially if patient is on psychotropics/antiseizure meds.
Prescribe only for identified needs (e.g., vitamin D supplementation if low; treat comorbid conditions per indications). Explicitly note no medication treats IDD itself.
Arrange follow-up to review response to interventions, ensure referrals are completed, reassess safety/behavior, and adjust supports.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety netting, and plan.

nl_assertions: Agent explains IDD as a lifelong neurodevelopmental condition with childhood onset involving limitations in intellectual AND adaptive functioning.
Agent states diagnosis is based on functional assessment and history (including school/developmental records when available), not solely on a single lab test.
Agent elicits caregiver/patient goals and main challenges (school/work, daily living skills, behavior, safety) and incorporates them into the plan.
Agent discusses prognosis in terms of maximizing function and independence with tailored supports rather than cure.
Agent reviews red flags requiring urgent care: new developmental regression, seizures, acute severe behavioral change, or immediate safety risks (self-harm/violence/neglect/abuse).
Agent explicitly states no medication treats IDD itself; management focuses on supports, therapies, accommodations, and treating comorbidities.

Agent outlines referrals and what each will evaluate (e.g., developmental-behavioral pediatrics/neurology/psychology for assessment; speech/OT/PT; psychiatry for comorbidities; social work/community services).

Agent addresses possibility of an underlying genetic syndrome when suggestive features (e.g., macrocephaly, dysmorphism, family history) are present and explains why genetics referral/testing can clarify etiology and guide anticipatory care and family counseling.
If vitamin D is low, agent explains supplementation purpose (bone health/deficiency correction) and expected duration with recheck plan.
Agent provides lifestyle education: sleep hygiene/routine; nutrition and physical activity; addresses lipid/glucose abnormalities if present with appropriate counseling and follow-up.
Agent offers community resources/disability services and caregiver support options (school IEP/504, vocational support, respite, benefits navigation).
Before prescribing, agent performs an allergy check (critical).
Before prescribing, agent attempts interaction and contraindication checks when relevant; omission of these is non-critical but should reduce quality score.
Agent avoids stigmatizing language, uses person-first and developmentally appropriate communication, and checks understanding with teach-back or equivalent.

minimal_paths.description: Confirm IDD clinically, identify needs and red flags, address vitamin D deficiency and provide supports/referrals with safety checks before prescribing.
must_collect: childhood onset of developmental delays/learning difficulties
adaptive functioning limitations (communication, social, practical/daily living)
current supports/school or work functioning and accommodations
red flags: regression, seizures, acute severe behavior change, safety risks
family history and syndromic features (e.g., macrocephaly/dysmorphism)
steps: ASK: developmental + adaptive history, comorbidities, goals, red flags, family history, meds/allergies
ORDER_LAB: include vitamin D and basic screening labs
GET_RESULTS: identify low vitamin D (and any metabolic issues if present)
DIAGNOSE: IDD based on functional assessment/history; discuss possible genetic etiology if indicated
EDUCATE: diagnosis, prognosis, supports/therapies, referrals, red flags, lifestyle, community resources; explain vitamin D plan
PRESCRIBE: vitamin D supplementation if low; avoid implying it treats IDD

minimal_paths.description: Confirm IDD and provide education/referrals/support planning without prescribing (e.g., if labs normal or no deficiencies identified).
must_collect: childhood onset of developmental delays/learning difficulties
red flags: regression, seizures, safety risks
steps: ASK: developmental/adaptive history, goals, red flags, family history, meds/allergies

DIAGNOSE: IDD based on history/functional assessment

acceptable_variants.description: Agent performs safety checks earlier (e.g., allergy/interaction checks immediately after medication history) but still before any prescribing.
Agent prioritizes urgent evaluation when red flags are present (e.g., new regression or seizures) and defers routine counseling/prescribing until stabilized.
Agent adds targeted workup/referral emphasis (genetics referral) when macrocephaly/family history/dysmorphism is elicited; may omit routine labs if recent results available.
